nativeRange.habitat: The Mystery Wrasse is native to the central western Pacific, where it lives on outer reef slopes and among deeper rubble and cave habitats. It is typically found between 20 and 58 meters, though it has been recorded at depths approaching 100 meters.

decorPreferences: abundant live rock with caves and overhangs
open swimming space along the front of the tank
fine sand bed at least 2–3 cm deep

diet.notes: Has been documented as a benthic invertebrate hunter in the wild, taking small crustaceans, commensal flatworms and pyramidellid snails. In captivity generally accepts vitamin-enriched frozen mysis and brine shrimp, finely chopped meaty seafood (squid, clam), and good-quality marine pellets and flakes. Tends to do best with several small feedings per day.

imageCandidates.notes: Erin Tomson; 3264x2448. Tack-sharp, vibrant full-body aquarium shot of a mature individual showing the reddish-pink body, yellow face, and fine vertical striations; tail ocellus obscured on this adult but ID unmistakable. Sharpest and most striking pick.
Goayzer; 4032x3024. In-tank reef shot under actinic/blue lighting clearly showing the diagnostic yellow-rimmed dark eyespot near the tail; slightly soft and colour-shifted by the blue light.

Roger Rittmaster; 1174x939. In-situ wild reef photo of a subadult with textbook yellow-rimmed tail ocellus and yellow face; slightly hazy underwater and lower resolution, kept as an ID-confirming extra.

summary: The Mystery Wrasse is a small, rarely encountered pygmy wrasse from the deeper reefs of the central western Pacific. Its reddish-pink body, thin white vertical bars, and a distinctive yellow-rimmed eyespot near the tail set it apart from other small wrasses kept in reef aquariums.
careNotes: Best housed in a well-established reef aquarium with abundant live rock, plenty of caves and overhangs for refuge, and open swimming space along the front. The Mystery Wrasse is initially shy and cryptic, often hiding for several days to weeks after introduction before settling in; a peaceful tank without overly aggressive tankmates is generally advised so that the new arrival has time to come out and feed. A tightly fitting lid is widely recommended, as Pseudocheilinus wrasses have been known to jump from open-top tanks. Like other pygmy wrasses, Mystery Wrasses tend to be strongly territorial toward conspecifics and toward other Pseudocheilinus (Six-line, Four-line, Eight-line) — they are best kept one per aquarium rather than in pairs or harems. Generally considered reef-safe and passive toward most other reef fish, the Mystery Wrasse cannot distinguish between desirable and undesirable invertebrates and has been reported to predate on small ornamental shrimp, pyramidellid snails, and commensal flatworms (which some keepers find useful for pest control). Like most wrasses, it is a protogynous (female-first) sequential hermaphrodite. Its native depth range of roughly 20–58 m places it in deeper reef habitat, so wild-collected specimens generally benefit from a gentle acclimation and somewhat subdued lighting at first.
breedingNotes: Captive breeding has not been meaningfully documented and is generally considered impractical in the home aquarium. The species is oviparous with distinct pairing during spawning, and is a protogynous hermaphrodite.

additional.name: Wikipedia (White-barred wrasse)
additional.notes: Cross-checked central western Pacific range (Japan to Coral Sea), max size 10.3 cm SL, depth 20–58 m, year of formal description (1999, Randall), and common-name synonymy (White-barred wrasse, White-barred pink wrasse, Mystery wrasse).

additional.name: Aquatics Unlimited
additional.notes: Secondary trade source. Cross-checked semi-aggressive temperament, reef compatibility, carnivorous diet, water parameters (72–78°F, pH 8.1–8.4, SG 1.020–1.025, KH 8–12), max size ~4 in, jumping risk (tight-fitting lid), one-per-tank rule for conspecifics, initial shyness. Suggested a smaller 75 US gal minimum tank — LiveAquaria's 90 gal was adopted per cautious-rounding rule.

fish.breedingNotes: Spawning has not been meaningfully achieved in home aquaria. The species is oviparous, forming distinct pairs to spawn in the wild, and is a protogynous hermaphrodite — meaning individuals are born female and may later transition to male.
